flashcards.back: The branch of statistics that deals with drawing conclusions about a population based on information obtained from a sample.
The entire set of all possible observations or measurements about which we want to draw conclusions.
A subset of the population selected for study, from which we make inferences about the entire population.
A numerical characteristic of a population, such as population mean (μ) or population variance (σ²). Usually unknown.
A numerical characteristic of a sample, such as sample mean (x̄) or sample variance (s²). Used to estimate population parameters.

flashcards.front: Why is statistical inference necessary in data science?
flashcards.back: With massive datasets, examining every data point is impractical. Statistical inference provides mathematical methods to make valid conclusions efficiently.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Descriptive and Inferential Statistics?
flashcards.back: Descriptive statistics summarizes data from a sample. Inferential statistics uses sample data to make predictions about the larger population.
A sample in which every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected, minimizing bias.
A sample that accurately reflects the characteristics of the population from which it was drawn.
Using sample data to calculate a single value (estimate) that serves as the best guess for an unknown population parameter.
Providing a range of values (confidence interval) within which the true population parameter is likely to fall.
A method to test claims about population parameters using sample data to determine whether to reject or accept a hypothesis.
The difference between a sample statistic and the true population parameter due to random selection of samples.

flashcards.front: What is the Central Limit Theorem and its importance?
flashcards.back: States that the sampling distribution of the mean approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, enabling inference about populations.

flashcards.front: What are the two main branches of Statistical Inference?
flashcards.back: Estimation (point and interval) and Hypothesis Testing.
